segments.s119-g17: بیامد is the simple past 3rd-person singular 'he came'. بگفت is the classical/poetic past 'he said' with the prefix be- (here functioning like 'and said' or simply 'said') introducing reported speech. آنچ is a relative/demonstrative 'that which' (introducing a content clause) and دید و شنید are past-tense verbs 'saw and heard' joined by و 'and'. The clause therefore means 'he came and said that which he saw and heard.'
segments.s119-g18: همه is a quantifier 'all' modifying پرده 'veil/curtain'. رازها is a plural genitive modifying پرده (literally 'the veils of secrets'). بردرید is the perfect/past 'tore apart' (برد- + رٍید), a transitive past verb; no explicit agent is required in classical narrative, so it reads 'all the veils of secrets were torn apart' or 'they tore apart all the veils of secrets.'
segments.s119-g19: چنین is an adverb 'thus/so' and گفت here introduces reported speech ('thus said'). کاو is an archaic/poetic pronoun meaning roughly 'he who' or simply 'he' (used as the subject of the reported clause). ز آسمان برترست is a comparative/locative clause where ز = 'than/from' and برترست is a present copula-form meaning 'is higher/ superior' (برتر + است). The clause reads 'Thus he said: he is higher than the sky/heavens.'
segments.s119-g20: نه is the negation 'not'. رای بلندش is a noun phrase 'his lofty counsel' (رای 'counsel/opinion' + adjective بلند 'lofty' with the possessive enclitic ش 'his'). به زیر is a prepositional phrase 'to/under the lower' and اندرست is an archaic present/copular construction 'is within / is subject to'. Together the clause means 'his lofty counsel is not under/subject to the low.'
segments.s119-g21: ز is the preposition 'because of/from'. گفتار من is the genitive 'my speech/words'. سر بپیچید is a past-tense verb phrase 'he turned his head away' (ب- prefix + پیچید, past 3rd sg. of پیچیدن 'to turn') and نیز is the adverb 'also/too' appended after the verb. The sequence means 'he turned his head away from my speech too.'
segments.s119-g22: جهان is the subject 'the world' (or 'this world'). پیش چشمش is a locative phrase 'before his eye / in his sight' (پیش 'before/in front of' + چشمش 'his eye'). نیرزد is a negative evaluative verb 'is not worth' (classical form related to ارزد), and به چیز is the complement 'a thing' in the idiom 'not worth a thing.' The clause means 'the world is not worth a thing in his sight.'
segments.s119-g23: جهاندار is the subject 'the sovereign' or 'king'. مر is an archaic possessive 'my' attached before the noun (mer = my). پهلوان را is the definite object 'the champion' marked by the object particle را. بخواند is a past/punctual verb with the be- prefix from خواندن; here it means 'summoned/called' (i.e., 'he called/summoned my champion').
segments.s119-g24: همه is the plural subject 'all/everyone'. گفت is the past 'said' introducing reported speech (the sense list provided lacks an explicit 'said' sense, so that entry is marked below as -1). The quoted clause فرهاد با او براند names Farhad (فرهاد) as the addressee/agent; با او 'with him' indicates accompaniment or instrumentality; براند is a present/jussive form of راندن (be- + راند) meaning 'may/must drive away' or simply 'drive away' in reported imperative. Overall: 'Everyone said, “Farhad, drive him away (with him).”'
segments.s119-g9: This is an elliptical prepositional/partitive phrase introduced by the poetic preposition ز (z), here meaning 'of/from' and used to present the quantity: 'of war‑elephants, twelve hundred'. The noun پیلان (pl. of پیل) is the head 'elephants' modified by the adjective جنگی 'war/military'; the number (هزار و دویست) is understood as the quantity being enumerated.
segments.s119-g10: که introduces a subordinate clause describing the effect/result: 'so that / such that...'. The locative phrase در بارگاه تو 'in your court/palace' places the scene; the existential predicate یک پیل نیست ('there is not one elephant') follows Persian word order with the numeral + noun before the copula/negator نیست.
segments.s119-g11: از ایران برآرم is a verb phrase with the preposition از + place 'Iran' and the first‑person singular present/future sense of برآرم 'I will raise / I raise'. یکی تیره خاک is a noun phrase 'one/dark soil' or 'one dark troop of the earth' (تیره here adjectival 'dark/ominous' modifying خاک 'earth/soil'), functioning as the object of برآرم.
segments.s119-g12: This clause predicates of the raised host. ندانند is a 3rd‑person plural present/epistolary form 'they do not know / they will not know'. بلندی here functions as an abstract verbal noun or infinitive meaning 'rising, getting up' (i.e. 'how to rise'), followed by باز از مغاک 'again/from the abyss' — together: 'they will not know how to rise again from the gulf/abyss.'
segments.s119-g13: چو بَشنید is a temporal clause: چو 'when' + بَشنید (past tense 'he heard'); the subject Farhad follows the verb (typical epic inversion). ازو داوری 'the judgment from him' is the object phrase: ازو 'from him' marks the source of the judgment/decision (داوری).
segments.s119-g14: This is a terse list of abstract nouns describing qualities: بلندی (here 'leaping/rising'), و تندی ('and fierceness/rapidity'), و کندآوری ('and slowing/deliberateness'). They function as accusative/complementary nouns that characterize the judgment or what Farhad heard; each noun is nominal and coordinated with و.
segments.s119-g15: بکوشید is past tense 'he strove/endeavored'. تا here means 'until' and introduces the result clause (بکوشید تا ... یافت): پاسخ‌نامه یافت 'he found an answer/response' — یافت is past tense 'he found'. The sequence shows effort (بکوشید) culminating in discovery (یافت).
segments.s119-g16: This clause describes the subsequent action: عنان ('the reins') is the object taken, سوی 'toward' introduces the goal, سالار ایران 'the commander/chief of Iran' is the target, and شتافت is the past tense verb 'he hastened/rushed'. The sequence is idiomatic: 'he took the reins and hurried toward the commander.'
segments.s119-g41: چنین گفت = 'he said thus' (چنین 'thus' placed before the verb for emphasis). کاین is an archaic contraction of 'که این' meaning 'that/this' introducing the reported clause. گفتن here is the verbal noun 'the saying' (the act/words being said) and نابکار is an adjective 'wicked' modifying that گفتن.
segments.s119-g42: نه خوب آید is a negated predicate: نه negates 'خوب آید' (= 'is not well/pleasing' — آید 'comes' used idiomatically for 'is fitting/pleasing'). از مردم هوشیار marks the group whose judgement matters ('from/among sensible people'); Persian uses از + noun to express 'to/for someone' in this evaluative sense.
segments.s119-g43: اگر سرکنی is a conditional protasis: اگر 'if' + سرکنی (2nd‑person subjunctive/optative of سرکن, literally 'you lift/raise your head' — here idiomatic). زین فزونی تهی is a short adjectival/ablative phrase 'empty of this excess' (زین 'this' used poetically, فزونی 'excess', تهی 'empty/devoid'), describing the state in which the conditional holds.
segments.s119-g44: به فرمان گرایی is a result clause where به + noun phrase indicates direction/tendency: 'you will go toward obedience/you will incline to command‑following' (گرایی here 'inclination/tendency'). بسان رهی is a simile 'like a wayfarer' modifying the manner; the copula/auxiliary ('you will be' / 'you will become') is implied rather than overt.
segments.s119-g45: وگرنه 'otherwise' begins the alternative clause. به جنگ تو لشگر کشم uses به جنگ 'to/into war' + تو (object of the threat) and لشگر کشم 'I will draw/bring an army' — کشم is 1st‑person form of کشیدن used here with a future/intentional sense (threatening). Word order places the target (تو) before the verb for emphasis.
segments.s119-g46: ز دریا به دریا marks a range 'from sea to sea' (ز ... به ...). سپه برکشم is the clause's verb phrase 'I will raise/bring a host/army' — برکشم is the compound with بر + کشم (first person), here used in the same future/intentional sense as above; the final نوشت (object) is سپه 'army'.
segments.s119-g47: روان بداندیش is a compound epithet 'one whose spirit/soul is ill‑thinking' = 'ill‑intentioned in spirit'. دیو سپید places the noun 'demon' before the adjective 'white' (سپید), so 'the white demon' (white‑haired / pale) is the subject described by the epithet.
segments.s119-g48: دهد is 3rd‑person present/subjunctive used as a simple present 'gives'. کرگسان را uses را to mark 'the vultures' as a definite object or affected party; به مغزت marks the content/target of the tidings ('concerning your brain' — مغزت = 'your brain'). نوید ('promise/omen/tidings') is the thing given, placed at the end in typical Persian order: 'gives the vultures tidings about your brain.'
segments.s119-g1: چنین (thus) is an adverb placed at the start to mark the manner of the reply; داد is the past‑tense 3rd‑person singular verb 'gave' (he gave); پاسخ is the direct object 'answer'; به کاووس is the indirect object 'to Kavus'. The final کی here is an archaic/poetic particle introducing the quoted clause (roughly 'saying' / 'who/that'), so the whole segment functions like 'he gave this answer to Kavus, saying...'.
segments.s119-g2: که‌گر is the fused conditional 'که گر' = 'if' that begins a subordinate clause; آب دریا is the noun phrase 'water of the sea'; بود is the copula in past/conditional sense 'were/was' (used here for a counterfactual or hypothetical); نیز is the adverb 'also'; می is the noun 'wine' (classical form). Grammatically this segment says the content of the reply: 'if the sea's water were also wine...'.
segments.s119-g3: مرا بارگه is the older possessive structure meaning 'my court' (مرا = 'my' before the noun, an archaic/genitive usage); زان تو functions as the comparative 'than you' (زان = 'from that' = 'than'); برترست is the predicate 'is superior'. Word order places the possessed noun before the comparative phrase and ends with the copular predicate.
segments.s119-g4: هزاران هزارم (literally 'thousands of thousands' or 'thousands upon thousands') expresses a very large number; فزون is an adjective/adverb 'more/abundant'; لشکرست is a nominal predicate 'is an army/host'. Together the clause says that his army numbers thousands upon thousands and is thus greater.
segments.s119-g5: به هر سو = 'to every side' / 'wherever'; که introduces a relative/locative clause 'that/where'; بنهند is 3rd‑person plural present/subjunctive of نهاد here meaning 'they set/they direct' (i.e. 'they face/turn'); بر جنگ روی literally 'their face toward war' (بر = 'toward', جنگ = 'war', روی = 'face'). The clause means 'wherever they set their face toward battle.'
segments.s119-g6: نماند is the negative perfect/past sense of ماندن 'was not left / did not remain'; به سنگ اندرون = 'in/within the stone' (به functioning as locative, اندرون = inside); رنگ و بوی = 'colour and scent'. The verb comes first (typical in poetry), followed by the locative and the things that ceased to remain: 'no color or scent remained within the stone.'
segments.s119-g7: بیارم is a first‑person verb 'I will bring / I bring' (biaaram/biaräm), here future/intent; کنون is the adverb 'now'; لشکری is a noun/adjective 'military, an army'; شیرفش is a compound epithet 'lion‑standarded' or 'with lion banners' (شیر = lion, فش/fash = flag/standard) describing the army. Note some tokens were split oddly in the provided list; grammatically the verb precedes the adverb and the noun phrase.
segments.s119-g8: برآرم is 1st‑person 'I will lift/raise' (here 'I will rouse'); شما را marks the direct object 'you (pl.)'; سر از خواب خوش literally 'head from pleasant sleep' is a poetic way to say 'from your sweet sleep' (i.e. rouse you from sleep). The verb+object+prepositional phrase order yields 'I will rouse you, lifting your heads from pleasant sleep.'
segments.s119-g33: شوم is a first‑person optative/subjunctive of شدن ('may I become / I shall become') used here to mean 'I will go / let me be'. چون introduces a simile 'as' and links to فرستاده‌ای, which functions as a predicative noun 'a messenger' (the -ای ending is the indefinite/agentive form). نزد + the enclitic pronoun اوی marks location/in the presence of 'him'. Word order is Verb (شوم) + simile phrase (چون فرستاده‌ای) + locative (نزد اوی).
segments.s119-g34: به گفتار uses به + noun adverbially = 'in/with (one's) speech' (here 'in words'). خون is the noun 'blood' modifying گفتار (i.e. 'words of blood'); اندر is the archaic/pre‑poetic preposition 'in/into'. آرم is first‑person singular (optative/present) meaning 'I put / I cast / I place' in a poetic register. به جوی is a final prepositional phrase 'into/toward the stream' (به = towards/into + جوی = stream). The clause therefore reads literally 'in speech of blood I put into the stream.'
segments.s119-g35: به پاسخ is a prepositional phrase 'in answer / in reply' (به used adverbially). چنین گفت is the formula 'thus said' (چنین = 'thus', گفت here functions as the past verb 'said' in narrative). کاووس شاه is the nominative name and title 'King Kavus' following the verb as a typical poetic word order (verb earlier, subject later).
segments.s119-g36: که introduces reported content ('that...'). از تو literally 'from you / on account of you' is used here with فروزد to indicate the person around whom the action turns (in context this yields the sense 'sell you'). فروزد is an archaic/poetic past/future‑oriented form from فروختن/فروزیدن; in this line it carries the sense 'sell' (i.e. someone will be sold). نگین و کلاه are the direct objects 'a gem and a cap', coordinated by و. The clause order is subordinate particle (که) + agent/related pronoun (از تو) + verb (فروزد) + objects.
segments.s119-g37: پیمبر تویی is a nominal sentence 'you are an envoy/messenger' with the copula embedded in the form تویی ('thou art'). هم تو repeats/emphasizes the subject 'you' (literary emphasis). پیل دلیر is a noun phrase 'a great/brave elephant' used metaphorically for a mighty warrior. Word order is predicative noun (پیمبر) + copula (تویی) then emphasis (هم تو) + appositive epithet (پیل دلیر).
segments.s119-g38: به هر کینه گه is a temporal/adverbial phrase: به هر = 'on/at every', کینه = 'enmity/grudge', گه = 'occasion' → 'at every outbreak/occasion of hatred'. بر before سرافراز شیر is a preposition 'upon/against' introducing the target 'the proud/triumphant lion'. The clause lacks an overt finite verb because the preceding nominal phrases supply the predication; the sequence means roughly 'you (act) upon the proud lion at every time of hatred.'
segments.s119-g39: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past verb 'he ordered/commanded/said' used for a ruler's speech or command. تا here is a subordinating particle with the sense 'so that / in order that' introducing the purpose/result clause. رفت is the past tense 'went'. پیشش is پیش + enclitic pronoun 'him' meaning 'before him / into his presence'. دبیر is the subject of رفت ('the scribe/secretary went forward'). Word order: verb (بفرمود) + purpose particle (تا) + verb (رفت) + locative/pronoun (پیشش) + subject (دبیر), which is a frequent poetic inversion.
segments.s119-g40: سر خامه را is a noun phrase 'the tip/head (سر) of the quill (خامه)' with را marking it as definite/affected object. کرد is the past tense 'he made / did'. پیکان تیر is a compound object 'an arrow (پیکان) of/— the shaft/bolt (تیر)' or simply 'an arrow'; together with کرد it means 'he made (it) an arrow' — i.e. he transformed the quill's tip into an arrow. The phrase illustrates a concise poetic transitive clause: object (سر خامه را) + verb (کرد) + result/object complement (پیکان تیر).
segments.s119-g25: 'چنین' (thus) is fronted as a discourse adverb modifying the speech; 'گفت' is the simple past 'said' introducing direct speech; the subject 'کاووس' follows the verb (verb–subject order, common in poetry); 'با پیلتن' indicates the addressee ('with Piltan' here functioning as 'to/with Piltan' — addressing him).
segments.s119-g26: 'کزین' = 'از این' = 'from/of this' (introduces the source or topic of the shame); 'ننگ' is the noun 'shame'; 'بگذارم' is first-person volitional/present-subjunctive ('I will put/leave/lay aside'), used here in a causative/active sense 'I will remove/rid (away)'; 'این انجمن' is the object phrase 'this assembly' that is affected. Word order is poetic: the source ('کزین ننگ') placed before the verb, and the affected entity ('این انجمن') comes after the verb.
segments.s119-g27: 'چو' introduces a temporal clause ('when'); 'بشنید' is the simple past 'he heard'; the name 'رستم' is placed after the verb (V+S order). 'چنین گفت باز' is the main clause: 'چنین' = 'thus', 'گفت' = 'said', and 'باز' = 'again' — the sequence emphasises that Rostam again spoke in the same manner.
segments.s119-g28: 'به پیش' = 'in the presence of / before'; 'شهنشاه' names the sovereign (the locus of the following action). 'کهتر نواز' is a compact phrasing: 'کهتر' (the lesser/inferior) modifies the verb/adjective 'نواز' (to treat kindly); here it functions as a directive or request that the person be treated more gently — the adjective-like verb 'نواز' is used in an exhortative/instructional sense in context.
segments.s119-g29: This clause uses an impersonal/modal construction: 'مرا برد باید' = 'it is necessary that I be taken / I must be taken' (literally 'me, to-take, must'), where 'مرا' is the accusative 'me', 'برد' is the verb 'to take/bring' in the verbal phrase, and 'باید' is the modal 'must/it is necessary'. 'بر او پیام' is a prepositional phrase 'a message to him' (literally 'upon/for him a message'), showing the purpose of the taking.
segments.s119-g30: 'سخن' = 'speech/words' is the direct object/topic; 'برگشایم' is first-person singular present/subjunctive 'I open/unveil/reveal' (here a promise to speak plainly); 'چو' = 'like/as' introduces the simile; 'تیغ از نیام' = 'a sword from its sheath' (i.e., the image of drawing a sword). Together the clause says the speaker will reveal words as decisively as drawing a sword from its scabbard.
segments.s119-g31: 'یکی' = 'one/a single' modifies 'نامه' (letter); the nominal phrase 'یکی نامه باید' uses 'باید' impersonal/modally to mean 'one letter is necessary'. 'چو' introduces a simile ('like/as'), 'برنده تیغ' = 'a cutting/sharp sword' where 'برنده' is an adjective 'sharp' modifying 'تیغ'. The whole says that a single, sharp letter is required.
segments.s119-g32: 'پیامی' = 'a message' (indefinite); 'به کردار' = 'in the manner/behaviour of' ('به' + noun 'کردار' functioning like 'in the manner of'); 'غرنده میغ' = 'roaring blade' where 'غرنده' (roaring) modifies 'میغ' (blade). The phrase characterises the message as fierce and cutting — delivered like a roaring blade.

sentences.s119-s9: He came and told what he had seen and heard; all the veils of secrets were torn apart.
sentences.s119-s10: Thus he said: 'He is above the heavens; his lofty counsel is not subject to the low.'
sentences.s119-s11: Because of my words he turned his head away as well; the world before his eyes is not worth a thing.
sentences.s119-s12: The sovereign summoned my champion; everyone said, 'Farhad, drive him away.'
sentences.s119-s5: I will bring up twelve hundred war‑elephants, so that not a single elephant remains in your palace.
sentences.s119-s6: From Iran I will raise a single dark host of earth; they will not know how to rise up again from the abyss.
sentences.s119-s7: When Farhad heard from him the judgment — (the) leaping, the fierceness, and the slowing —
sentences.s119-s8: He strove until he found an answer; taking the reins, he hastened toward the commander of Iran.
sentences.s119-s21: He said thus: that this saying of the wicked does not sit well with sensible people.
sentences.s119-s22: If you raise your head, emptied of this excess, you will incline to obey commands like a traveller.
sentences.s119-s23: Otherwise, I will lead an army against you in war; from sea to sea I will raise a host.
sentences.s119-s24: The ill‑intentioned spirit — the white demon — gives the vultures tidings (an omen) of your brain.
sentences.s119-s1: He thus answered Kavus: “If the water of the sea were also wine…”
sentences.s119-s2: My court is higher than yours; my thousands upon thousands are a far greater host.
sentences.s119-s3: Wherever they turned toward battle, no color or scent remained within the stone.
sentences.s119-s4: I will now bring an army bearing lion‑standards; I will rouse you, lifting your heads from sweet sleep.
sentences.s119-s17: I will go to him as a messenger, and with words of blood I will cast (them) into the stream.
sentences.s119-s18: In reply King Kavus said thus: 'He will sell you for a gem and a cap.'
sentences.s119-s19: You are a messenger; you are also (even) a mighty, brave elephant — at every outbreak of hatred you fall upon the proud lion.
sentences.s119-s20: He commanded, so the scribe went before him; he turned the tip of the quill into an arrow.
sentences.s119-s13: Kavus said thus to Piltan: “I will rid this assembly of this shame.”
sentences.s119-s14: When Rostam heard this he again spoke, and before the king he urged gentler treatment.
sentences.s119-s15: I must be brought to him with a message; I will unfold my words like a sword from its sheath.
sentences.s119-s16: One letter is needed — like a keen sword; a message in the manner of a roaring blade.
